content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 207 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Toll-free Assistance • • • Disqualification • • • Executive Fund • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5860            322-5640              322-5711              322-6441            322-6444


December 21, 1978


78-12-137

Stephen Chace Bass, Chair
Long Beach Housing Action
Association
2625 E. Third Street, Room 24
Long Beach, CA 90814

Dear Mr. Bass:

Thank you for your letter of November 24, 1978 requesting advice with respect to the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act. I hope the following advice is helpful.

Enclosed please find a copy of a recent Commission opinion analyzing the disqualification provisions of the Act in the context of a proposed rent control ordinance in Los Angeles.

You asked whether a person who owns a one tenth interest in a ten unit building would be disqualified from voting on a rent control measure. Assuming that the measure foreseeably would materially affect the income producing or fair market value of the building, the official with such an interest would be disqualified. The Commission's opinion provides that persons owning three or fewer units of residential rental property are not part of the rental property industry but that persons owning four units or more of residential property are part of the industry. In the instant case, the official is a question is a part owner in a building with more than four units. As such, he and his partner are part of the rental property industry.

The fact that his ownership share of the building is less than four units does not alter the fact that the building in which he is an investor is large enough to make it and its owners part of the industry.

[illegible] 11-2

Stephen Chace Bass
December 21, 1978                                                Page Two


     Second, you asked whether an official has a financial
interest in investments or interests in real property held
by the spouse or dependent children. Government Code Sections
92029 and 87103 both provide that for disclosure and dis-
qualification purposes the official has a financial interest
in the investments and interests in real property of his spouse
and dependent children.

     If you have any further questions, please contact me.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        [illegible]
                                        Ted Pinn, Chief
                                        Conflicts of Interest Division

Enclosure
TP:nrw

2625 E. Third St. Rm 24
Long Beach, CA 90814
November 24, 1978

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Sir/Madam

The Long Beach Housing Action Association is a community organ-
ization in Long Beach working to improve housing conditions in
our city. We are currently engaged in an effort to secure the
passage of a rent rollback and moritorium ordinance in Long
Beach so that tenants may share in the tax savings of Proposition
13.

In the course of our research on this issue, we have attempted
to determine the eligibility of the individual City Council
members who own rental property to vote on any proposed rent
rollback ordinance when it is presented to the City Council.
According to our investigation, only one Council member, Mayor
Thomas Clark, has any direct interest in rental property. Mr.
Clark owns one-tenth interest in a ten unit apartment building
in Long Beach (please see enclosed letter from the City Clerk).

The Long Beach Housing Action Association would appreciate the
Fair Political Practices Commission providing us with a ruling
or opinion as to whether or not Mr. Clark is entitled to vote
on any form of a rent rollback ordinance under the guidelines
you have established. We would also appreciate knowing whether
or not the fact a Councilman's spouse or other member of his
or her family owns rental property would constitute a conflict
of interest for that Councilman.

We would appreciate receiving your answer to the above inquiries
by December 8 since we intend to bring the rent rollback issue
before the City Council December 12. Thank you in advance for
your consideration and assistance.

Respectfully,

Stephen Chace Bass, Chair
Long Beach Housing Action Association

CITY OF LONG BEACH

ELAINE HAMILTON                                    Long Beach, California 90802
CITY CLERK


October
Nineteenth
1
9
7
8


Mr. Sid Solomon
6317 Eliot Street
Long Beach, California 90814

Dear Mr. Solomon:

In reply to your communication regarding whether Long Beach
City Councilmembers would have to abstain from voting on a
rent issue, please be advised that we have checked the latest
financial statements filed by the individual Councilmembers
and found that none of them own more than three apartments
with the exception of Mayor Clark who owns a one-tenth inter-
est in a ten unit apartment building at 2391 Locust Avenue.

I cannot advise you as to what the legal interpretation of
this ownership would be, however, I assume it would mean he
would own one unit.

If you require further information or have any questions,
please contact us.

Very truly yours

[signature]
ELAINE HAMILTON
City Clerk

EH:ha

Third Street, Room 24 Long Beach, CA 90814 Dear Mr. Bass: Thank you for your letter of November 24, 1978 requesting advice with respect to the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act. I hope the following advice is helpful. Enclosed please find a copy of a recent Commission opinion analyzing the disqualification provisions of the Act in the context of a proposed rent control ordinance in Los Angeles. You asked whether a person who owns a one tenth interest in a ten unit building would be disqualified from voting on a rent control measure. Assuming that the measure foreseeably would materially affect the income producing or fair market value of the building, the official with such an interest would be disqualified. The Commission's opinion provides that persons owning three or fewer units of residential rental property are not part of the rental property industry but that persons owning four units or more of residential property are part of the industry. In the instant case, the official in question is a part owner in a building with more than four units. As such, he and his partners are part of the rental property industry. The fact that his pro rata share of the building is less than four units does not alter the fact that the building in which he is an investor is large enough to make it and its owners part of the industry. Second, you asked whether an official has a financial interest in investments or interests in real property held by the spouse or dependent children. Government Code Sections 82029 and 87103 both provide that for disclosure and disqualification purposes the official has a financial interest in the investments and interests in real property of his spouse and dependent children. If you have any further questions, please contact me. Sincerely, Ted Prim, Chief Conflicts of Interest Division Enclosure TP:nrw 2625 E.
